1: Who is this coming from Edom, with garments reddened from Bozrah — this one glorious in his attire, striding forward in his great strength? Bozrah was the Edomite capital (modern Buseira in Jordan). Edom in prophetic literature (Obad; Jer 49; Ezek 35) was the representative enemy who exploited Jerusalem's fall in 586 BCE, blocking fugitives and celebrating the destruction (Obad 11-14; Psalms 137:7). The divine warrior arriving from Edom with blood-stained garments is returning from executing judgment on Israel's most treacherous historical neighbor.
2: Why are your garments red, as if you had been treading in a winepress? The question from the observer establishes the interpretive frame: the red garments need explanation. Winepress treading was a communal harvest activity where workers stripped off their robes and tread the grapes barefoot — the grape juice stained everything red. The winepress image for judgment appears in Joel 3:13 and Revelation 14:19-20, which quotes this passage directly.
3: I have trodden the winepress alone, and from the nations no one helped me — I trod them in my anger and trampled them in my fury; their blood is sprinkled on my garments, and I have stained all my clothing. The divine warrior's solitary action echoes 59:16 and Isaiah 63:5: YHWH looked for a human helper and found none, so his own arm acted alone. The identification of the grape-juice stains with blood reframes the winepress as a battle: the nations are the grapes, their blood the divine warrior's harvest. The sprinkle (yazzeh) echo connects to the Servant's sprinkling of nations in Isaiah 52:15.
4: For the day of vengeance was in my heart and the year of my redemption had arrived. The pairing of vengeance-day and redemption-year is the same dual action as Isaiah 61:2 (year of favor + day of vengeance). The divine warrior's inner conviction — carried in his heart through the period of Israel's suffering — now becomes outward action. The year of redemption (shenat ge'ulai) is the Jubilee vocabulary again: the appointed time when the kinsman-redeemer acts.
5: I looked, but there was no one to help — I was appalled that there was none to sustain me; so my own arm brought salvation, and my fury sustained me. The third iteration of the no-human-helper motif (cf. Isaiah 59:16; here again). YHWH's appallment (va'eshtomaim) at the absence of helpers is a divine emotional response to human absence — not weakness but the appropriate reaction to finding a partner-shaped void. His own arm and his own fury become the substitutes for the human partnership that would have been the fitting response.
6: I trampled the peoples in my anger — I made them drunk in my fury, and I poured their blood down to the ground. The winepress metaphor completes: the nations made drunk with the divine fury-wine are the grapes crushed in the press. Pouring blood to the ground is both the ritual of blood-disposal (Leviticus 17:13 — blood poured on the ground as the soul returned to earth) and the image of total defeat. The divine warrior's comprehensive victory leaves no enemy standing.
7: I will recount the steadfast lovingkindnesses (hasde YHWH) of the LORD and his praises — for all that the LORD has bestowed on us, the great goodness to the house of Israel according to his compassion and his abundant steadfast love. The transition from the divine warrior's victory speech to historical confession and lament is abrupt but structurally coherent: the God who just acted alone in judgment is also the God of Israel's covenantal history. The plural hasde (loyal covenant acts in the plural) catalogs the accumulated divine faithfulness across generations.
8: For he said, 'Surely they are my people, children who will not prove false' — and so he became their Savior. YHWH's initial confidence in Israel at the Exodus election is recalled here: the covenant-establishing declaration that these are his people. The expectation that they will not prove false (lo yeshaqeru) is the covenantal assumption that was historically violated. YHWH's savior-role follows from his election-declaration: having claimed them as his, he is bound to save them.
9: In all their affliction he was afflicted, and the angel of his presence saved them — in his love and compassion he redeemed them; he lifted them up and carried them through all the days of old. The angel of his presence (mal'ach panav) is the personified divine presence in the Exodus pillar — the representation of YHWH's face going with Israel. The identification of YHWH with Israel's suffering (be-khol tzaratam lo tzar) is one of the most striking expressions of divine empathy in the entire OT: his people's adversity was his own adversity. He carried them through the wilderness period as a father carries a child (Deuteronomy 1:31).
10: But they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit — so he turned against them as their enemy and himself fought against them. The first explicit reference to grieving the Holy Spirit in Scripture (Ephesians 4:30 echoes this directly: do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God). YHWH's response to grief — turning against them as their enemy — is the covenant-curse in its most personal form: the one who was their protector becomes their opponent. The divine warrior who fought for them now fights against them.
11: Then he remembered the ancient days, the time of Moses and his people. Where is the one who brought them up out of the sea with the shepherd of his flock? Where is the one who placed his Holy Spirit among them? The lament-question addresses YHWH's own apparent memory lapse: where is the God of the Exodus? The shepherd of the flock is Moses (or YHWH as Moses's principal); the Holy Spirit placed among the community is the divine presence that accompanied the wilderness community. The question implies: that God still exists — why has he not acted?
12: Who led Moses by the right hand with his glorious arm — dividing the waters before them to make an everlasting name for himself, who led them through the depths? Like a horse in the desert, they did not stumble. Leading Moses by the right hand is the intimate guidance image: YHWH directly steering the leader as he steers a child learning to walk. The divided sea and the depths-crossing establish the Exodus as YHWH's name-making event — his reputation among the nations was built on this act. The horse-in-desert image captures confident, unimpeded movement in apparently impassable terrain.
13: Who led them through the depths — like a horse in the desert, they did not stumble. As cattle descend into a valley to rest, the Spirit of the LORD gave them rest. The Spirit as the agent of the Exodus rest-giving (the cloud by day and fire by night providing the community's orientation and rest) is now invoked as precedent for the Spirit's present activity. The cattle-descending-to-valley image is the comfortable, natural movement of domesticated animals toward their resting place: the Spirit made the wilderness feel like home.
14: As cattle descend into a valley to rest, the Spirit of the LORD gave them rest — so you led your people to make yourself a glorious name. The Exodus series of Spirit-acts (dividing sea, leading through depths, giving rest) culminated in YHWH's name being made glorious among the nations. The lament implicitly asks: do the same name-making acts again — demonstrate your power now as you did then, so that your name is again glorified in the sight of the nations watching Israel's exile.
15: Look down from heaven and see, from your holy and glorious dwelling. Where are your zeal and your strength? The yearning of your heart and your compassion are withheld from me. The lament-invocation requesting divine attention from the heavenly throne room echoes Psalms 80:14. YHWH's zeal (qin'ah) — the passionate, jealous protection of covenant relationship — and his hamah (yearning of the bowels = visceral compassion) are appealed to directly. The complaint that these are withheld is not accusation but petition: the one who withholds has the power to release.
16: For you are our Father, though Abraham does not know us and Israel does not acknowledge us — you, LORD, are our Father; our Redeemer from of old, that is your name. The appeal to divine fatherhood over biological ancestry is remarkable: even if Abraham and Jacob (Israel) could not recognize the exilic community as their heirs, YHWH still owns the relationship as Father. Father is both intimate and covenantal — the term used by the returning prodigal and the title by which Israel addressed YHWH in covenant prayer (cf. Jeremiah 3:19). Redeemer from of old grounds the appeal in YHWH's historical identity, not a new claim.
17: LORD, why do you let us stray from your ways and harden our hearts from fearing you? Turn back for the sake of your servants, the tribes of your inheritance. The theological boldness of the petition — asking YHWH why he permitted the apostasy that led to the exile — is the lament's most daring claim. The hardening of the heart is attributed to divine permission (cf. the hardening of Pharaoh's heart), not to autonomous human choice alone. Turning back (shuv) addresses both YHWH's return to the community and the community's return to YHWH — the two movements of covenant restoration are inseparable.
18: Your holy people possessed your sanctuary for a little while — now our adversaries have trampled it down. The brevity of the sanctuary's period of Israelite possession is the complaint: from Solomon's dedication (957 BCE) to the Babylonian destruction (586 BCE) was approximately 370 years. But measured against YHWH's eternal ownership of the holy mountain, this was brief. The trampling of the sanctuary by adversaries — the very place where YHWH's name dwelt — is the most acute form of covenant humiliation.
19: We have become like those you never ruled, like those who were never called by your name. The exile community's self-description as equivalent to the uncovenant pagans is the lament's sharpest formulation: the distinction between Israel and the nations has been erased by the catastrophe. Being called by YHWH's name (niqra shemkha alenu) was the covenant identity marker — to be like those who were never called by his name is to have lost the entire basis of covenant identity. The lament ends here, preparing for the theophany-prayer of chapter 64.
